subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7! In this unit you will learn all about time – how we measure it, name it, and use it to plan our day. Time is passing all around you, every moment. The sun rises and sets, the days of the week follow one another, and the months roll by until a whole year has gone past. When Amina waits for the Maghrib prayer, when Yusuf counts the days until Eid, or when Halima checks the calendar to see how many weeks are left until Ramadan, they are all thinking about time.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does time matter so much? Time helps us organise our whole life. It tells the trader in the Hargeisa market when to open the stall and when to close it. It tells a farmer near Kismaayo when to plant and when to gather the harvest. It tells every Muslim family the five times each day to pray, because the prayer times follow the movement of the sun through the sky. When you become confident with time, you can plan your day, keep your promises, and never be late for the things that matter.

As you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and if you can, a calendar and a clock or watch beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Say the days of the week out loud, point to today's date on a calendar, and time yourself doing small tasks. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Little by little, time will become one of your very best friends.
Name the units of time – second, minute, hour, day, week, month, year – and put them in order from shortest to longest.
Say how the units are related, such as 60 seconds = 1 minute and 7 days = 1 week.
Name the days of the week in order and the months of the year in order.
Read and understand a calendar, and find a date on it.
Write a date in numbers (05/06/2022) and in words (Sunday 5th June 2022).
Work out yesterday, today and tomorrow from any given day.
Estimate how long an activity will take, then measure it and compare.

concepts.title: The Units of Time
concepts.explanation: We measure time using different units, from very short ones to very long ones. Just as we use centimetres for short lengths and kilometres for long journeys, we use small units of time for quick things and big units for long stretches of time. Let us meet each one. A second is the shortest unit you will use. It is very quick – about the time it takes to clap your hands once, blink your eyes, or say the word ‘Bismillah’. When Farah times a fast race, she counts in seconds.
concepts.example: A minute is longer than a second. In fact, 60 seconds make one minute. A minute is about how long it takes to brush your teeth, tie your shoelaces, or wash your hands properly before eating. It is a handy unit for short…

concepts.title: Ordering Units of Time
concepts.explanation: Notice how each unit is bigger than the one before it. A second fits inside a minute, a minute fits inside an hour, an hour fits inside a day, and so on, all the way up to a year. It is like a set of nesting bowls: the smallest bowl fits inside a slightly bigger one, which fits inside a bigger one still. Knowing this order helps you answer many questions. If Hassan asks, ‘Which is longer, a week or a day?’ you look at the order and see that week comes after day, so a week is longer. If Khadija asks, ‘Which is…
concepts.example: You can practise this order out loud like a little chant: ‘second, minute, hour, day, week, month, year.’ Say it forwards and then backwards until it stays firmly in your memory. Sagal likes to say it while walking to…

concepts.title: How the Units Are Related
concepts.explanation: These relationships let you answer real questions. If Omar knows that 1 minute is 60 seconds, then 2 minutes must be 60 + 60 = 120 seconds. If Zahra knows that 1 week is 7 days, then 2 weeks must be 7 + 7 = 14 days. You simply add the same amount again for each extra unit. This is a first taste of the multiplying you will do in later units – for now, adding the amount again is perfect. It also works the other way. If Idris has 14 days, he can group them into sevens: 7 days and 7 days make 2 weeks. If Maryam counts…
concepts.example: These relationships let you answer real questions. If Omar knows that 1 minute is 60 seconds, then 2 minutes must be 60 + 60 = 120 seconds. If Zahra knows that 1 week is 7 days, then 2 weeks must be 7 + 7 = 14 days. You…

concepts.title: The Days of the Week
concepts.explanation: There are 7 days in a week, and each one has its own name. Here they are in order: The days always follow this order, and then they start again. After Sunday comes Monday once more, round and round, week after week. This is called a cycle – the days repeat in a never-ending ring of seven. If today is Friday, then in exactly seven days it will be Friday again.
concepts.example: Some days have special jobs. Monday to Friday are usually school days and working days, when Nuur Academy is open and the markets are busy. Saturday and Sunday together make the weekend, a time for rest, family and…

concepts.title: The Months of the Year
concepts.explanation: There are 12 months in a year, and like the days, each one has a name and a fixed order. Here they are: January, February, March (the start of the year)
concepts.example: October, November, December (the end of the year)

concepts.title: Reading a Calendar
concepts.explanation: A calendar is a chart that shows the days, weeks and months of a year. It helps us keep track of dates and plan ahead. Every home benefits from a calendar: it shows when the weekend falls, when a birthday is coming, and how many days remain until Eid. A calendar page for one month has these parts:
concepts.example: The name of the month at the top (for example, MARCH).

methods.title: How to order units of time
methods.example: Put these units in order from shortest to longest: week, second, hour, day, minute.
steps: Picture the full order: second, minute, hour, day, week, month, year.
Pick out only the units you were given.
Keep them in that same order, shortest first.

methods.title: How to write a date in words
methods.example: Match each on the left to its equal on the right: 1 minute, 1 hour, 1 day, 1 week.
steps: Read the day number, then add its ending (st, nd, rd, th).
Name the month from its number (1 = January … 12 = December).
Add the year. Include the day of the week if you know it.

methods.title: How to estimate and measure time
methods.example: Fill in the gaps: Monday, ___, Wednesday, ___, Friday, ___, ___.
steps: Think of a similar activity you have done before.
Make a sensible estimate.
Measure the real time with a clock or stopwatch.
Compare your estimate with the real time.

methods.title: Missing months of the year
methods.example: Which month comes after July, and which month comes before April?
steps: Solution: Say the months in order.
After July comes August.
Before April comes March.
Saying the list in order is the surest way to answer.

methods.title: Read a calendar
methods.example: Using the March 2023 calendar above, what day of the week is March 22nd?
steps: Solution: Find 22 in the grid, then follow its column up to the day names.
The 22 sits in the ‘We’ column (with 1, 8, 15, 22, 29), so March 22nd is a Wednesday.
Check the read a calendar result carefully.

methods.title: Write a date in words
steps: Solution: The 30 is the day, 11 is the month (November), and 2022 is the year.
So in words it is 30th November 2022.
Remember the ‘th’ ending on the 30.

workedExamples.title: Order the units of time
workedExamples.prompt: Put these units in order from shortest to longest: week, second, hour, day, minute.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Think of the full order – second, minute, hour, day, week, month, year. Pick out only the ones we were given, keeping that order: second, minute, hour, day, week. The second is shortest and the week is longest.

workedExamples.title: Match the time relationships
workedExamples.prompt: Match each on the left to its equal on the right: 1 minute, 1 hour, 1 day, 1 week.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 1 minute = 60 seconds; 1 hour = 60 minutes; 1 day = 24 hours; 1 week = 7 days. Learn these four facts and many time questions become easy.

workedExamples.title: Missing days of the week
workedExamples.prompt: Fill in the gaps: Monday, ___, Wednesday, ___, Friday, ___, ___.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Say the days in order to find each gap. After Monday comes Tuesday; after Wednesday comes Thursday; after Friday come Saturday and Sunday. The full week is Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday.

workedExamples.title: Missing months of the year
workedExamples.prompt: Which month comes after July, and which month comes before April?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Say the months in order. After July comes August. Before April comes March. Saying the list in order is the surest way to answer.

workedExamples.title: Read a calendar
workedExamples.prompt: Using the March 2023 calendar above, what day of the week is March 22nd?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find 22 in the grid, then follow its column up to the day names. The 22 sits in the ‘We’ column (with 1, 8, 15, 22, 29), so March 22nd is a Wednesday.

workedExamples.title: Write a date in words
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The 30 is the day, 11 is the month (November), and 2022 is the year. So in words it is 30th November 2022. Remember the ‘th’ ending on the 30.

workedExamples.title: Write a date in numbers
workedExamples.prompt: Write Saturday 25th December 2021 in numbers as DD/MM/YYYY.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The day is 25, December is the 12th month, and the year is 2021. So we write 25/12/2021. December already has two digits (12), and 25 has two digits, so no extra zeros are needed.

workedExamples.title: Yesterday and tomorrow
workedExamples.prompt: If today is Wednesday, what day was yesterday and what day will tomorrow be?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Step back one day for yesterday: Tuesday. Step forward one day for tomorrow: Thursday. So yesterday was Tuesday and tomorrow will be Thursday.

workedExamples.title: Order by duration
workedExamples.prompt: Put these in order from shortest to longest: sleeping at night, clapping your hands once, eating breakfast, reading a chapter.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Think about how long each takes. Clapping (about 1 second) is shortest, then eating breakfast (a few minutes), then reading a chapter (longer), then sleeping at night (many hours) is longest. The order is: clapping your hands once, eating breakfast, reading a chapter, sleeping at night.

workedExamples.title: Plan within a time
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf has 1 hour (60 minutes) of free time. He wants to read a book (20 minutes), draw a picture (15 minutes), and do a puzzle (10 minutes). Does it all fit?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the times: 20 + 15 + 10 = 45 minutes. Since 45 is less than 60, it all fits, with 60 − 45 = 15 minutes to spare. Good planning leaves a little time over.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: If today is Wednesday, name yesterday and tomorrow.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Write Saturday 25th December 2021 in numbers as DD/MM/YYYY.

activities.title: Make Your Own Calendar
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, a pencil, coloured pencils.
steps: Draw a grid with 7 boxes across (one for each day) and 5 rows down.
Write the day names along the top: Mo Tu We Th Fr Sa Su.
Fill in the dates for this month, starting on the correct day.
Colour the weekends and mark any special days, like a birthday or Eid.
Keep your calendar on the wall and cross off each day as it passes.

activities.title: Time a Task (Stopwatch Fun)
activities.materials: a clock, watch, or phone that shows seconds, and a partner.
steps: One person picks a task: counting to 20, saying the alphabet, or standing on one foot.
The other person says ‘GO!’ and starts timing, then stops when the task is finished.
Write down how many seconds it took.
Swap over and try again. Did the time change the second time?
This activity, based on the ‘Time a Task’ project, helps you feel how long a second and a minute really are.

activities.title: Estimate Then Measure (Investigation)
activities.materials: a timer and a list of small tasks.
steps: Before you start, estimate how long each task will take (for example, writing your name, tidying your books, walking to the gate).
Now measure the real time with the timer.
Compare your estimate to the real time. Were you close?
Do it again with new tasks. Do your estimates get better with practice?

activities.title: Days and Months Memory Game
steps: Write each day of the week on a card, and each month of the year on another card.
Shuffle the day cards and race to put them back in the right order.
Do the same with the month cards. Time yourself and try to beat your record.
Ask a family member to remove one card while you close your eyes – can you say which is missing?

activities.title: The Yesterday–Today–Tomorrow Game (Family Game)
steps: One person calls out a day, for example ‘Thursday’.
The next person must quickly say yesterday (Wednesday) and tomorrow (Friday).
If they are right, they call out the next day for someone else.
Play faster and faster to make it exciting.

activities.title: My Daily Timeline
steps: Draw a long line across your paper for one day, from waking up to sleeping.
Mark on the five prayer times and your main activities (school, meals, play).
Next to each, write about how long it takes – in minutes or hours.
Show your timeline to your family and talk about how you spend your time.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand time best? Were your estimates close to the real times? What would you like more practice with – the calendar, the units, or estimating? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Units of Time Order Shortest to longest: second, minute, hour, day, week, month, year. Each unit is bigger than the one before it.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Time Relationships 60 seconds = 1 minute. 60 minutes = 1 hour. 24 hours = 1 day. 7 days = 1 week. 12 months = 1 year. 365 days = 1 year.

rules.title: Calendar Rule
rules.text: To find the day for a date, follow the date's column UP to the day names. The same weekday repeats every 7 dates down a column.

terms: The short units: 60 seconds = 1 minute, 60 minutes = 1 hour
A chart of days, weeks and months
A day named by number, month and year
commonMistakes: Chant: second, minute, hour, day, week, month, year
February has 28 (29 in a leap year); use the rhyme
Follow the date's column UP to the day names

realProblems.prompt: Ramadan lasts about 30 days. About how many weeks is that? (7 days make a week.)
realProblems.answer: 1) About 4 weeks (30 ÷ 7 is about 4, with 2 days over).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) About 4 weeks (30 ÷ 7 is about 4, with 2 days over).

realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Sumaya has 1 hour before iftar. She helps for 25 minutes chopping, 15 minutes washing rice, and 10 minutes setting the mat. Does it all fit, and how much time is left?
realProblems.answer: 25 + 15 + 10 = 50 minutes, which fits in 60, leaving 10 minutes.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 25 + 15 + 10 = 50 minutes, which fits in 60, leaving 10 minutes.

games.title: Quick Match: Second / Minute / Hour
games.description: Practise second / minute / hour through four short challenges.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does time matter so much? Time helps us organise our whole life. It tells the trader in the Hargeisa market when to open the stall and when to close it. It tells a farmer near Kismaayo when to plant and when to gather the harvest. It tells every Muslim family the five times each day to pray, because the prayer times follow the movement of the sun through the sky. When you become confident with time, you can plan your day, keep your promises, and never be late for the things that matter.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and if you can, a calendar and a clock or watch beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Say the days of the week out loud, point to today's date on a calendar, and time yourself doing small tasks. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Little by little, time will become one of your very best friends.
I can name the units of time – second, minute, hour, day, week, month, year – and put them in order from shortest to longest.
I can say how the units are related, such as 60 seconds = 1 minute and 7 days = 1 week.
I can name the days of the week in order and the months of the year in order.
I can read and understand a calendar, and find a date on it.
I can write a date in numbers (05/06/2022) and in words (Sunday 5th June 2022).
